slides.title: Banking ROE Competitive Benchmarking Analysis: A Five-Year Strategic Performance Review (2021-2025)
slides.subtitle: A Comprehensive Comparative Study of Return on Equity and Return on Tangible Equity for Bank ABC, First Abu Dhabi Bank, and Gulf International Bank.

slides.title: Executive Summary: FAB Leads Regional Performance with 20.5% ROTE in H1 2025

elements.heading: Strategic Leadership in Capital Efficiency and Shareholder Value Creation
elements.body: The competitive benchmarking analysis compares the Return on Equity (ROE) and Return on Tangible Equity (ROTE) of Bank ABC, First Abu Dhabi Bank (FAB), and Gulf International Bank (GIB) from 2021 to 2025, focusing on shareholder return efficiency and capital performance. FAB leads with a strong and consistent ROTE increase, reaching about 20% by mid-2025, driven by diversified income streams, AI-driven operational efficiencies, disciplined cost management, and international expansion. Bank ABC shows modest ROE improvement but lacks ROTE disclosure, limiting full performance assessment; its ROE remains below sector benchmarks due to a large equity base diluting returns and foreign exchange volatility impacting profits. GIB maintains stable but lower ROE and ROTE, with good liquidity and risk management but limited growth and innovation compared to peers. External factors such as Basel III regulatory requirements, regional economic growth, and fintech competition influence all banks, with regulatory capital buffers supporting resilience but constraining ROE. Strategic recommendations include Bank ABC enhancing capital allocation and transparency, GIB diversifying income and investing in technology, and FAB expanding AI initiatives and maintaining revenue strategies to sustain its competitive advantage. Overall, ROE and ROTE remain critical metrics for evaluating bank profitability and capital efficiency, with FAB exemplifying best practices in maximizing shareholder returns amid evolving market and regulatory conditions.

slides.title: Strategic Significance of ROE and ROTE as Modern Performance Indicators

items.title: Profitability and Capital Efficiency
items.description: ROE measures how effectively a bank generates profit from its equity base, serving as a direct gauge of shareholder value creation. ROTE refines this by excluding intangible assets, offering a more precise view of returns on tangible capital. This distinction is vital for investors who prioritize the actual physical and financial assets that generate core banking income without the noise of goodwill or brand valuation. By focusing on these metrics, institutions can better understand their internal rate of return and the efficiency of their capital deployment strategies across various business lines and geographic regions.

items.title: Comparative Benchmarking
items.description: These metrics enable meaningful comparisons across banks with differing capital structures, asset compositions, and business models. ROTE, in particular, helps normalize differences arising from intangible asset levels, which can vary significantly between legacy institutions and those that have undergone recent mergers or acquisitions. This normalization allows analysts to strip away accounting artifacts and focus on the underlying operational performance of the bank, ensuring that comparisons between institutions like FAB and Bank ABC are grounded in tangible financial realities rather than accounting discrepancies.

items.title: Strategic Insight and Risk
items.description: Trends in ROE and ROTE over time can reveal shifts in business strategy, risk appetite, and operational efficiency. For example, improvements may indicate better cost control, revenue diversification, or enhanced asset quality. Conversely, a declining trend might signal increasing competitive pressure, rising credit costs, or an inefficiently large capital base. By tracking these metrics over a five-year horizon, stakeholders can identify whether a bank's strategic initiatives are translating into sustainable financial performance or if the institution is merely benefiting from temporary macroeconomic tailwinds.

items.title: Investor and Regulatory Focus
items.description: Investors and regulators closely monitor ROE and ROTE as indicators of financial resilience and sustainable profitability. High and stable ROE/ROTE levels often correlate with strong capital management and competitive advantage. Regulators use these metrics to assess the long-term viability of a bank's business model, while investors use them to determine the attractiveness of the bank's stock. In an era of increasing regulatory scrutiny under Basel III and SREP, maintaining healthy returns while meeting stringent capital requirements has become the ultimate test of banking management excellence.

slides.title: Methodology and Data Integrity: Ensuring Transparency in Benchmarking

steps.label: Phase 1
steps.title: Data Sourcing and Verification
steps.description: The analysis draws on official financial statements and investor presentations published by Bank ABC, FAB, and GIB, including annual reports and interim results through Q3 2025. Regulatory and supervisory disclosures, such as Basel III capital ratios, were utilized to ensure consistency in capital definitions across all three institutions. Industry reports from McKinsey and Reuters provided secondary verification.

steps.label: Phase 2
steps.title: Calculation and Normalization
steps.description: ROE was calculated as Net Profit Attributable to Shareholders divided by Average Total Equity. ROTE was derived by deducting Intangible Assets from Total Equity to isolate tangible capital returns. Average equity was calculated as the simple average of opening and closing equity for each period. FX effects were adjusted where banks reported underlying net profit on a constant currency basis.

steps.label: Phase 3
steps.title: Temporal Scope and Trend Analysis
steps.description: The analysis covers the five financial years from 2021 through 2025, including interim data up to Q3 2025. This period captures the post-pandemic recovery, regional economic shifts, and strategic transformations. The inclusion of interim data allows for the identification of intra-year trends and provides the most current insights available as of the December 2025 reporting cycle.

slides.title: Comparative Performance Benchmarking
slides.subtitle: Analyzing ROTE and ROE Trends Across the GCC Banking Landscape (2021-2025)

slides.title: Comparative Performance Data: ROTE and ROE Metrics (2024-2025)
Strategic Positioning Matrix: Return Magnitude vs. Volatility
Bank ABC: Capital Efficiency and Profitability Analysis

items.heading: Modest ROE Improvement Trends
items.body: Bank ABC does not publicly disclose ROTE figures in its financial releases for 2021–2025. However, ROE can be approximated using reported net profit and equity data. For example, in full year 2024, net profit was US$285 million and equity stood at US$4,207 million, implying an approximate ROE of 6.8%. In H1 2025, net profit was US$152 million and equity was US$4,363 million, implying an approximate annualized ROE of around 7.0%. These figures suggest modest improvement in ROE from 2024 to 2025, though still below typical banking sector benchmarks. The absence of ROTE data limits direct comparison between tangible and total equity returns.

items.heading: Core Business Revenue Drivers
items.body: Several factors influence Bank ABC’s ROE trajectory: strong core business growth and diversified revenue streams supported net profit increases. Equity rose from US$4,207 million at end-2024 to US$4,363 million by mid-2025. The bank has focused on its wholesale and Islamic banking segments to drive top-line growth, while its digital retail arm, ila Bank, continues to gain traction in the Bahraini market. These diversified streams provide a buffer against localized economic downturns, though the overall return on the massive equity base remains a challenge for management as they seek to optimize capital allocation across their international footprint.

items.heading: FX Volatility and Translation Impacts
items.body: Foreign exchange translation effects, particularly the depreciation of the Egyptian Pound (EGP) and Brazilian Real (BRL), significantly impacted both net profit and equity. While underlying constant-currency net profit growth was a robust 9% in H1 2025, the headline figures were dampened by these currency headwinds. Managing a global footprint requires sophisticated hedging strategies, yet the sheer scale of currency devaluation in key markets like Egypt has made it difficult to fully insulate the ROE from these external shocks. This volatility remains a primary concern for shareholders looking for consistent dollar-denominated returns.

items.heading: Robust Capital and Liquidity Buffers
items.body: Capital and liquidity buffers remained robust, with Tier 1 ratios around 15% and CET1 around 13%. While these strong ratios support financial stability and regulatory compliance, they also dilute ROE by increasing the equity base. Bank ABC maintains LCR and NSFR metrics well above regulatory minima, ensuring it can withstand significant market stress. However, the trade-off for this safety is a lower return on equity compared to peers who may operate with leaner capital structures. The strategic challenge lies in maintaining this resilience while finding ways to deploy excess capital into higher-yielding assets.

slides.title: First Abu Dhabi Bank: The Regional Leader in Capital Returns
Gulf International Bank: Balancing Stability with Profitability Challenges

left.heading: Profitability Drivers and Strengths
left.body: Gulf International Bank (GIB) has demonstrated a consistent ability to maintain a relatively high ROE compared to regional peers, although the trend has shown a gradual decline from its 2021 peak of 20.6%. The bank's net interest income grew by 5% in 2024, reaching USD 522.2 million, while net fee and commission income saw a significant 19% increase. This successful diversification of income streams has been a cornerstone of GIB's resilience. Furthermore, improved risk management led to a sharp decline in provisions, which dropped from USD 112 million in 2023 to USD 54.9 million in 2024, directly boosting the bottom line and supporting equity returns. The bank's focus on wholesale banking and its strong presence in the Saudi market continue to be its primary engines of growth, providing a stable foundation for its capital efficiency metrics.

right.heading: Strategic Challenges and Weaknesses
right.body: Despite its strengths, GIB faces a declining ROE trend, which fell to approximately 17.3% by mid-2025. This pressure on profitability is partly due to a rapidly expanding equity base, which rose to USD 2.6 billion by Q3 2025. Without a corresponding acceleration in net income, this rising equity naturally dilutes ROE. Additionally, GIB does not publicly disclose ROTE figures, which limits the ability of investors to assess the impact of intangible assets on its capital efficiency. The bank also faces the challenge of balancing its conservative risk appetite with the need for innovation-driven growth. As regional competition intensifies from larger players like FAB and agile fintech entrants, GIB must find ways to optimize its cost structure and accelerate its digital transformation to prevent further erosion of its return metrics and maintain its competitive standing in the GCC banking sector.

slides.title: Strategic Drivers and External Factor Assessment
slides.subtitle: Evaluating the Impact of Regulation, Macroeconomics, and Competitive Dynamics

slides.title: Drivers of Performance Variance: FAB vs. Bank ABC and GIB

left.title: FAB: Scale and Innovation Advantage
points: Diversified revenue mix with 43% non-funded income contribution.
Advanced AI integration (Agentic AI) driving operational efficiency.
Significant international revenue growth (17% of group total).
Lean cost-to-income ratio of 22.3% through digital automation.
Strategic restructuring into four high-performance divisions.

right.title: Bank ABC/GIB: Structural and Regional Headwinds
points: Significant ROE dilution due to large, under-utilized equity bases.
High exposure to FX volatility in emerging markets (EGP, BRL).
Lower non-interest income diversification compared to FAB.
Higher compliance and operational costs in fragmented markets.
Limited scale in high-margin digital retail segments.

slides.title: External Factor Impact: Regulatory and Macroeconomic Pressures

items.text: The UAE banking sector operates under a robust regulatory framework aligned with Basel III standards. As of end-2024, UAE banks maintained an average Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 13.8%, significantly above the minimum requirement of 7%. These strong buffers support resilience but may slightly dilute ROE by increasing the equity base relative to risk-weighted assets, creating a permanent drag on return metrics.

GDP growth in the GCC is projected at approximately 3 percent in 2025, rising to 4.1 percent in 2026, driven by infrastructure investment and non-oil sector expansion. While this provides a supportive backdrop for loan growth, the overall profitability of the sector remains tied to oil price stability and the successful execution of national diversification strategies like Saudi Vision 2030 and UAE 'We the UAE 2031'.

Monetary easing has compressed net interest margins (NIMs) across the region. In H1 2025, NIMs declined from 2.8 percent to 2.6 percent year-on-year, and further rate cuts in late 2025 are expected to continue this trend. The UAE Central Bank’s base rate cut to 3.90 percent in October 2025—the lowest since 2022—lowers borrowing costs but also pressures interest income and overall ROE.

The competitive landscape is evolving rapidly, with digital transformation and fintech innovation reshaping banking dynamics. Banks are investing heavily in digital platforms and AI to maintain margins. For example, Commercial Bank of Dubai achieved a high ROE of 23.48% by rolling out Microsoft Copilot, demonstrating that technology adoption is no longer optional but a primary driver of capital efficiency.

Federal Decree Law No. 6 of 2025 significantly expands regulatory enforcement powers in the UAE, including fines up to AED 1 billion and mandatory ESG integration. These measures raise compliance costs and operational risk, which may exert downward pressure on ROE if not managed efficiently. Banks must now balance the costs of sustainability reporting with the need to maintain high shareholder returns.
